questions.question: What syntax is used for pseudo-classes?
questions.explanation: Pseudo-classes use a single colon (e.g., :hover, :first-child). Double colons (::) are used for pseudo-elements.

questions.question: What is the correct order for link pseudo-classes?
questions.explanation: The LVHA order (Link-Visited-Hover-Active) ensures all states work correctly without being overridden.

questions.question: What does :nth-child(odd) select?
questions.explanation: :nth-child(odd) selects children at odd positions (1, 3, 5, ...). This is equivalent to :nth-child(2n+1).

questions.question: What is the difference between :first-child and :first-of-type?
options: :first-child selects first child regardless of type; :first-of-type selects first of its element type
:first-of-type selects first child regardless of type
Both select only the first element
questions.explanation: :first-child targets an element that is the first child of its parent. :first-of-type targets the first element of its type among siblings.

questions.question: What specificity do pseudo-classes have?
questions.explanation: Pseudo-classes have the same specificity as regular classes: 0,1,0.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class selects elements that are NOT matching a given selector?
questions.explanation: The :not() pseudo-class excludes elements that match its argument selector.

questions.question: What does :empty select?
options: Elements with no children or text
questions.explanation: :empty selects elements that have no children (including text nodes). Whitespace is considered content.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class is triggered when an element is the target of a URL fragment?
questions.explanation: :target selects the element whose ID matches the URL's fragment identifier (the part after #).

questions.question: What does :focus-visible do differently from :focus?
options: It only shows focus for keyboard users, not mouse clicks
It only shows focus for touch users
It shows focus only on mobile
questions.explanation: :focus-visible shows a focus indicator only when the browser determines it's needed (usually keyboard navigation), not on mouse clicks.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class selects the root element of the document?
questions.explanation: :root selects the highest-level parent element in the DOM. For HTML documents, this is the <html> element.

questions.question: What does :nth-child(2n+1) select?
questions.explanation: 2n+1 produces odd numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7... This is equivalent to :nth-child(odd).

questions.question: Which pseudo-class applies to form elements with valid data?
questions.explanation: :valid selects form elements whose content validates successfully against the input constraints (e.g., type, required, pattern).

questions.question: What does :checked select?
options: Radio buttons or checkboxes that are selected
questions.explanation: :checked selects radio buttons or checkboxes that are in the checked state.

questions.question: How can you select an element that does NOT have a class?
questions.explanation: element:not([class]) selects elements that do not have a class attribute. The :not() pseudo-class combined with attribute selector.

questions.question: What is the :where() pseudo-class used for?
questions.explanation: :where() groups selectors like :is() but contributes zero to specificity, making it useful for default styles that should be easy to override.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class is used to style an element when it's being activated (clicked)?
questions.explanation: :active styles an element during the moment it's being activated (e.g., the instant between mouse press and release).

questions.question: What does :last-of-type select?
options: The last child of any type
The last element of its type among siblings
The last element in the document
The last attribute of an element
questions.explanation: :last-of-type selects the last element of a particular type among its siblings.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class represents a disabled form element?
questions.explanation: :disabled selects form elements that have the disabled attribute, making them uneditable.

questions.question: What is the specificity of :is(.class1, .class2)?
questions.explanation: :is() takes the specificity of its most specific argument. Since both arguments are classes, it's 0,2,0.

questions.question: What does ::selection (pseudo-element) vs :target (pseudo-class) do?
options: ::selection styles highlighted text; :target targets URL fragment
::selection is for print; :target is for screen
questions.explanation: ::selection is a pseudo-element that styles text selected/highlighted by the user. :target is a pseudo-class that targets the element matching the URL fragment.
